← the Atlas

Review orchestration — kill-rounds beat debates

analysis · budding ·implemented ·JUN 11, 2026

An 18-agent research pass over the multi-agent-review literature and kolu's debate skills, proposing the next /be-review — lens before codex, a shared findings ledger, capped evidence-gated debates, cold cross-family verification of unanimous findings, and a kill-round quick tier.

Contents

  • TL;DR
  • What the evidence says
    • Diversity, not debate, is where the measured payoff lives
    • More rounds make consensus worse, not better
    • For review, the best-evidenced shape is a cold kill-round, not a conversation
    • Redaction is the cheapest large lever
    • Structural-lens diversity is a bet, not a result
  • The design tournament
    • Why debate-centric lost (3–3.5/10)
    • Why workflow-maximalist lost (4.5/10)
    • Why ledger + kill-round won (7/10, both judges)
  • The proposed /be-review
    • Reorder: lens before codex
    • One findings ledger across all four tracks
    • Tier the gauntlet: kill-round by default, debate where contested
    • Honest, substituted degradation
    • Rationale into adjudication, not generation
    • What changes in /be itself
    • Shipping order
  • The constituent skills
    • /codex-debate: cap the review loop, gate concessions on evidence
    • /lens-debate: verify unanimity, tighten the backstop

How should /be’s review gauntlet be orchestrated, now that it has two Workflow-backed debates (/codex-debate, /lens-debate), a second model family on tap, and #1269 ‘s answer mode shipping the symmetric contract? This note answers that question from evidence and proposes the next /be-review. The research was commissioned against the sibling problem — agency’s /do reviewer, which kolu consumes via srid/agency#master (apm.yml:24) — but the ingredients are identical (two Claude lenses plus codex over a live diff), and the conclusions are about orchestration shape, not the host skill. The upstream /do redesign is out of scope here; this note reads the same results back onto /be and /be-review. 18-agent workflow + adversarial critique

Method: a Claude Code dynamic workflow ran 4 parallel evidence gatherers (kolu’s debate internals, agency’s documented reviewer failure history, Workflow-tool capabilities, the academic/practitioner literature), then 3 independent designers from different angles, 9 adversarial critics (cost, quality, portability — three per design), and 2 judges with opposing priorities (merged-code quality vs. pragmatic adoption). Roughly 1.1M subagent tokens. Both judges independently ranked the same design first.

TL;DR

Implementation status

Shipped in #1277 (the same PR as this note): the gauntlet reorder + rationale rerouting, the write-ahead findings ledger + gauntlet badge, and the round backstops + cited concessions + tiered codex effort in both debate skills. Deliberately not shipped: the --quick kill-round tier and the unanimity cross-family check — both remain gated on the retrospective A/B over past merged PRs (see Shipping order), per this note’s own evidence discipline.

Verdict

Spend debate where disagreement is; spend verification where agreement is. The measured payoff of multi-model review comes from model diversity, not from the debating — and rounds past the first measurably degrade outcomes (sycophantic convergence, bias amplification, unanimous-wrong consensus). For /be-review that means five changes: (1) run lens before codex, so the cross-family debate reviews the lens’s structural rewrites — today nothing cross-model ever does; (2) share one findings ledger across all four tracks (stable ids, cross-track dedup, crash-resume, deterministic comments); (3) cap both debates and gate concessions on cited evidence — #1222 is the in-house preview of what the literature predicts; (4) verify lens unanimity with one cold codex kill-round — agreement between two same-family lenses is the correlated, low-signal kind and currently applies with zero adversarial check; (5) thread rationale into adjudication, not generation — pre-loading reviewers with author intent is the measured 3–4× finding-suppression channel, and the curation bias /lens-debate’s own #1109 trial discovered independently.

The proposed gauntlet:

QuestionAnswerStrongest evidence
Does Claude ⇄ codex debate improve review?The second model family improves it; the debating mostly doesn’tReConcile attributes gains to diversity, not protocol; independent aggregation beat debate in a Du-et-al follow-up
How many rounds are worth it?One cold cross-check round; gains saturate at two; three is harmfulUnanimous-wrong consensus grows monotonically with rounds (~24% of disputed items by round 3)
What kills false positives?Cold, claims-only adversarial verification with mandatory code citationsRefute-or-Promote killed 79–83% of candidate findings as false positives pre-disclosure
Is the lens cross-examination worth keeping?Yes — cross-lens checking has the only empirically validated catch in the stack; keep it for contested findingsagency#198 caught a real cross-lens bug; /lens-debate’s #1109 trial validated independent-then-debate
Biggest cheap lever?Redaction: never show reviewers the author’s self-assessment or the proposer’s reasoningAuthor framing cut findings 3–4× in a controlled bug-planting study

What the evidence says

Four research threads, fact-checked against primary sources where the claims are load-bearing. Secondhand numbers (search-summary provenance) are flagged; directions are corroborated across multiple sources even where exact figures are not.

Diversity, not debate, is where the measured payoff lives

The foundational multi-agent-debate result (Du et al. 2023) shows 3 agents × 2 rounds with majority vote beating single-model chain-of-thought on math, factuality, and MMLU. But the controlled replication literature is sobering:

  • Smit et al., “Should we be going MAD?” found multi-agent debate does not reliably outperform self-consistency or ensembling at comparable cost without careful tuning. The most promising knob was agreement modulation — controlling how easily agents concede.
  • A controlled study of MAD in logical reasoning found debate adds value only when agents initially disagree; when initial answers are similar, extra rounds add cost, not signal.
  • “Debate or Vote” decomposes MAD into its parts and finds majority voting is the main contributor — most of the gain comes from aggregating independent agents (the pre-debate ensemble), not from the debate rounds themselves. (Note that Diversity of Thought is not counter-evidence here: it shows debate helps — a diverse medium-capacity set reaches 91% on GSM-8K vs. 82% for three copies of one model — but its point is that the leverage is model diversity within debate, which is the same Claude+codex premise below.)
  • ReConcile — a round-table of heterogeneous models with confidence-weighted voting, up to +11.4% over baselines — explicitly attributes its gains to model diversity, not the discussion protocol. This is the strongest academic support for the Claude+codex premise: the payoff comes from the second model family, and most of it is collectable without any debate at all.

More rounds make consensus worse, not better

This is the part that cuts directly against “no round cap, no deadlock exit”:

  • A 2026 study of multi-agent deliberation (arXiv 2604.07667) reports that by round 3, ~24% of initially-disputed questions end in unanimous wrong consensus, increasing monotonically with rounds. “Not All Flips Are Conformity” decomposes stance changes: strict conformity (~29% of flips) is predominantly harmful (57–77% correct→wrong), and agents adopt errors even from vacuous reasoning 20–39% of the time. (Both secondhand; the direction is corroborated.)
  • Judging with Many Minds (EMNLP 2025 Findings) found multi-agent debate amplifies judge biases sharply after the first round (position, verbosity, bandwagon) and the amplification persists; meta-judge setups resist bias better than debate. Survey literature agrees gains saturate at round 2, marginal at round 3.
  • LLM-as-judge baselines are themselves shaky: position bias flips pairwise code judgments on order swap more than 10% of the time (Judging the Judges); self-preference bias correlates with self-recognition (NeurIPS 2024) — a direct argument for cross-family review over same-family second passes.
  • Structural judgments have no oracle — and the domains without checkable answers are precisely where deliberation converged wrong most often. The lens debates operate exactly there.

Worth stating carefully: /lens-debate’s convergence argument (the section now titled “Why convergence, not deadlock, is the expected outcome”, .apm/skills/lens-debate/SKILL.md) is about termination — disinterested lenses with no authorship stake do converge. The literature’s complaint is about correctness: they converge, and a measured fraction of the time they converge wrong, at a rate that grows with rounds. Termination and truth are different properties; a round cap defends the second, not the first.

For review, the best-evidenced shape is a cold kill-round, not a conversation

The single most relevant paper to this design space is Refute-or-Promote, a stage-gated adversarial pipeline for defect discovery:

  • Creative agents argue a finding is real; cold-context adversarial agents receive only the claim — not the proposer’s reasoning — and try to kill it; nothing ships without an empirical check.
  • Results: 79–83% of candidate findings were false positives killed before disclosure. The cross-model critic stage caught bugs same-family review missed and found correctness issues in ~16% of same-family-approved fixes.
  • Its hardest-won lesson: “unanimity is a low-signal event.” 80+ agents unanimously endorsed a nonexistent OpenSSL vulnerability (killed by one test), and a unanimous kill of a real bug later became a CVE. Agreement between LLMs is not evidence; only execution is.

Supporting results: Cross-Context Review found fresh-session review beats same-session (anchoring suppresses criticism) and cross-model beats same-model even at equal capability. MAD applied directly to code tasks shows mixed results, and heterogeneous agent teams often underperform their best member due to consensus-seeking over expertise.

Redaction is the cheapest large lever

A controlled bug-planting experiment (Claude reviews codex’s work, 96 reviews) found framing is the biggest quality lever: passing the implementer’s confident self-assessment to the reviewer cut findings 3–4× (9.4 → 2.4–4.0 per review) and halved critical-severity tags. Redaction discipline mattered more than which vendor reviewed.

kolu independently discovered the same effect: /lens-debate’s #1109 trial found that feeding hickey a pre-curated lowy finding to rebut produced a drop verdict, while independent parallel review made hickey raise the same issue on its own and flip the verdict to fix — “curation biases the outcome; independent-then-debate does not” (.apm/skills/lens-debate/SKILL.md). The literature and the in-house experiment agree. The design consequence: redact the author’s self-assessment and confidence language from generation prompts, and redact the proposing reviewer’s chain of reasoning from verification packets — but always include the full claim text and its cited code locations, or responsible refutation becomes impossible.

Structural-lens diversity is a bet, not a result

Cross-model diversity for bug-finding is real: CodeFuse-CR-Bench finds no single LLM dominates across code-review dimensions; vendor benchmarks claim multi-model review catches ~1/3 more issues with low overlap. But no study directly measures whether frontier models produce different structural findings (decomposition, coupling, volatility) — and a strong lens prompt like hickey/lowy’s may homogenize outputs across model families, shrinking the diversity you’re paying for while inflating false confidence when both families “agree”. The honest position: codex running the hickey lens is an unproven bet; codex verifying hickey-lens claims is the evidenced role. This is testable cheaply — run the kill-round retrospectively over ~10 past merged PRs and count verified refutes/amends/duplicates before building anything permanent on the generation side.

The design tournament

Three designers worked from the same four evidence briefs under different angles — minimal-change incrementalist, workflow-maximalist, debate-centric — each producing a complete orchestration design for the same ingredients /be-review orchestrates: two Claude lenses plus codex over a live diff. Nine adversarial critics attacked each design through one lens each (cost/latency/maintenance, review quality, portability/failure-modes); two judges scored the dossier with opposing priority orderings. Both judges picked the same winner. The tournament ran against the upstream /do reviewer, but every verdict below is about the orchestration shape itself.

Why debate-centric lost (3–3.5/10)

The most architecturally elegant reading of the debate literature — debate only on disagreement, claims-only challenges, progress-gating — but its central mechanism failed both judges’ first criterion:

  • Its corroboration gate auto-applied precisely the findings most likely to be correlated false positives (same diff, same lens catalog, two model families sharing the prompt) with less scrutiny than the status quo, while “unanimity is a low-signal event” says agreement must never reduce scrutiny.
  • Its contested→No-op disposition reinstalled Defer through the back door, with the incentive gradient pointing exactly opposite to the hard-won flip-to-Fix rule (three generations of prompt patching upstream: #55 → #159 → #160 ): refute-then-stall costs two cheap calls; confirming obligates an apply+commit+verify chain.
  • Its cost and quality premises were mutually exclusive: high cross-family finding overlap makes codex generation pure waste; low overlap routes most findings into per-finding debates with serialized warm sessions — re-deriving the #1222 runaway with a termination proof attached.

Why workflow-maximalist lost (4.5/10)

The most complete mechanization toolkit (schema’d dispositions, role separation in code, the only design confronting the re-review hole head-on) — but as specced:

  • ~19–22 agents and 3–5 codex execs per run, on what is already among the slowest review steps in measured timing data ( #128 );
  • a round-1 gate that hard-failed the whole step on a single codex flake, directly contradicting its own soft-degradation story;
  • a stability loop that licenses lens ping-pong and manufactures convergence via gag-order prompts;
  • a ~10-artifact maintenance surface across three languages with the findings schema duplicated — the mirror-edit failure mode kolu’s own codex-debate history warns about, shipped twice.

Why ledger + kill-round won (7/10, both judges)

The winning design structures the data instead of rebuilding the orchestration: a findings ledger every stage writes, one cold cross-model kill-round replacing the weakest cross-check, debates confined to where disagreement actually is.

  • The codex bet is bounded: codex acts only on refute/amend/duplicate verdicts; confirms change nothing versus the status quo; codex absence degrades to the existing baseline verbatim. Worst case approximates today.
  • Cost explosion is structurally impossible: no uncapped loops anywhere; fixed agent count; the zero-findings path is cheaper than today.
  • Independently-landable increments — the proven parts ship before the bets.
  • Its critiques found genuine criticals — but every one converged on a point-fix (write-ahead ledger, symmetric evidence gate, resequencing) rather than an indictment of the architecture.

The proposed /be-review

What /be-review (.apm/skills/be-review/SKILL.md) should become. The gauntlet’s two best properties are untouched: the serial sole-editor contract (each step reads a settled tree — collisions stay impossible) and the deferred-comments contract (push once, then post; no comment ever advertises a local-only commit).

Reorder: lens before codex

Today’s order is codex → lens → simplify → police, and the SKILL itself documents the property that makes this order wrong: “each step sees the commits the previous step added… a later reviewer reviews the earlier reviewer’s fixes too.” That free re-review pass is currently pointed away from the riskiest commits in the run. /lens-debate applies structural rewrites — decomposition changes, boundary moves, the largest diffs any reviewer produces — and in today’s order nothing cross-model ever reviews them: only /simplify and /code-police follow, and neither is a cross-family correctness reviewer. Flipping to lens → codex → simplify → police makes the codex debate cover the lens’s applied refactors, closing the unreviewed-refactor-commits hole using machinery /be-review already has. The serial-editor argument is order-independent; only the coverage changes. (Corollary: the codex debate’s round-1 packet should not be told which commits came from the lens step — cold review of the whole diff, per the redaction evidence.)

One findings ledger across all four tracks

Four tracks currently post four comments and nothing dedups across them — codex and lens can flag the same construct, and police’s elegance pass re-fixes what simplify settled. A run-scoped write-ahead ledger (.be-review/ledger.json, gitignored, the .codex-debate/ scratch-dir pattern generalized) fixes this and two other problems at once:

  • Stable finding ids (track-prefixed: lens:lowy-1, codex:F2, police:P1 — recorded per the SKILL’s ledger rules), with exact-location cross-track dedup — a later track references an existing finding instead of re-raising it as a new row.
  • Crash-resume. The ledger is stamped with the merge-base sha and run id, persisted after every track and after each applied commit (sha recorded with the finding, plus machine-readable provenance trailers on the commits themselves). An interrupted gauntlet — today a 4-track, possibly hour-long run with no written non-clean-exit contract — resumes by content instead of re-running tracks against a tree that already contains their fixes.
  • Deterministic comments. The post-push comments render from the ledger (the renderLedger pattern debate.workflow.js already uses in-process) instead of each track’s body being held in main context across the entire gauntlet and retyped at the end.
  • Mode badges. The comment header states the regime the run actually got — full gauntlet / quick tier / codex-degraded — so review-depth variance is visible on the PR instead of three indistinguishable regimes shipping under identical comment formats.

Tier the gauntlet: kill-round by default, debate where contested

The full gauntlet runs two debates at Opus/xhigh per PR. The evidence-shaped cheap tier (--quick, and worth considering as the default for routine changes): independent lens generation (parallel, exactly as /lens-debate already does) → merge with stable ids → one cold codex verification of the merged findings — a claims-only packet; codex returns confirm / refute / amend / duplicate per finding, refutes requiring a file:line citation that a read-only check verifies against the tree → apply the survivors → simplify → police. Fixed agent count, one codex exec, zero debate rounds. The dispositions are computed by the gate, not negotiated:

  • verified refute → dropped, evidence recorded in the ledger;
  • unverified refute → the fix ships, row marked contested with codex’s objection verbatim — apply-and-flag, never silently dropped (a drop-on-assertion disposition would be Defer wearing a schema);
  • amend → the remedy text is replaced before apply — this verdict is where “would applying this fix create a problem under the other lens?” lives in the quick tier;
  • duplicate → cross-lens dedup for free.

The full debating gauntlet remains for flagship changes — and the ledger makes the escalation signal mechanical: a quick-tier run that comes back with contested rows is the run that earns the debate.

Honest, substituted degradation

Today, three reviewer-error attempts mean “give up on codex… move on” — the run silently loses its only cross-family check, with one report line. Degradation should be monotone and visible: on persistent reviewer-error, substitute a fresh-context same-family correctness pass (fresh-session still beats same-session per Cross-Context Review — strictly better than nothing), and badge the final comments with the degraded mode. A flaky codex should never yield less review than an absent one, and the human reading the PR should be able to tell which regime ran.

Rationale into adjudication, not generation

/be §4 passes the change rationale “so the lenses don’t flag deliberate decisions” (.apm/skills/be/SKILL.md), and /be-review threads it into /lens-debate. But author intent and confidence shared with reviewers is the measured 3–4× finding-suppression channel, and pre-loading reviewers is exactly the curation bias the #1109 trial proved changes verdicts. The deliberate-decision context belongs at the disposition step: let the lenses flag the deliberate decision cold, then let the rationale justify a disposition during cross-examination — visibly, in the debate trail, instead of invisibly, as a finding that was never born. Same data, one phase later, on the record.

What changes in /be itself

  • §4: invoke the reordered gauntlet; pass rationale with the new meaning (adjudication-phase context). The unresolved-lens-finding adjudication duty is unchanged — it just arrives better-labelled, from the ledger’s contested rows.
  • §0 interview: no new question by default. Tier selection (quick vs. full) should be automatic — diff size, task kind from the existing interview, presence of structural rewrites — with the interview asking only when genuinely ambiguous, consistent with §0’s don’t-pad rule.
  • §5/Done: the gauntlet outcome line gains the mode badge (which tier ran, whether codex degraded), so the final report states the review depth actually delivered.

Shipping order

  1. Reorder the tracks + reroute the rationale — prose-only edits to be-review/SKILL.md and be/SKILL.md §4; immediate coverage win, zero new machinery.
  2. The ledger — scratch-dir WAL + deterministic comment rendering; benefits every later step and gives the gauntlet crash-resume.
  3. Caps + concession schemas in the two debate workflows (below) — small workflow.js and schema edits.
  4. The quick tier + unanimity kill-round — one new codex-crosscheck-style script on the existing codex-exec-lib.sh plumbing, gated on a retrospective A/B over ~10 past merged kolu PRs (count verified refutes/amends/duplicates) so the codex-verdict bet is measured before it’s trusted.

The constituent skills

The gauntlet redesign needs two upgrades in the debate skills it composes. kolu’s stack is ahead of the field in three ways the evidence endorses — structured JSON consensus detected in code (not vibes), kernel-enforced read-only codex sandboxes, and /lens-debate’s independent-then-debate discipline, which independently replicated the published anchoring results. Everything below keeps those.

/codex-debate: cap the review loop, gate concessions on evidence

  • Adopt a round backstop in review mode. /lens-debate already has the right shape — --max-rounds with unresolved as a first-class terminal state that /be-review knows how to adjudicate. Review-mode /codex-debate has neither (“no round cap, no deadlock exit”), and #1222 documents the consequence. The literature adds the sharper point: even converging debates go increasingly wrong after round 2. A backstop of 3 with unresolved reported honestly is not a deadlock exit — quitting without agreement and pretending otherwise is what defeats the purpose; surfacing genuine disagreement to the human is the correct output of a debate that didn’t converge.
  • Evidence-gated concessions. Require a cited reason whenever a disposition flips across rounds — and enforce it in the workflow, don’t just ask. A single persistent per-finding gate holds a finding OPEN while a citation is outstanding from either side, so it ends unresolved rather than counting as consensus. An uncited author flip (disputed → fixed/partial with no concessionReason) owes a debt that survives across rounds — dropping the action or holding the flip doesn’t launder it. The reviewer side needs the same workflow check, not just the schema: concession being required in codex’s --output-schema enforces only that the string is present, not that it’s non-empty when codex resolves by accepting a dispute (a cross-field condition no JSON Schema can express), so the workflow holds a finding codex marks resolved off an author dispute with an empty concession OPEN too. Smit et al. found agreement modulation is the highest-leverage MAD knob, and the conformity literature shows ungated flips are predominantly harmful. A side that cannot cite why it changed its mind hasn’t been convinced; it has capitulated to end the loop.
  • Tier the reasoning effort. xhigh on round 1, high thereafter — the #1222 fix list already proposed this; agency#97 establishes high as the find-real-issues threshold for codex reviews.
  • Answer mode ( #1269 ) shares the loop-to-agreement contract and should get the same two guards (backstop + concession reasons); its parallel-swap confirmation phase is sound and unaffected.

/lens-debate: verify unanimity, tighten the backstop

  • Unanimity is the unguarded path. Findings both lenses agree on in round 1 lock as settled and get applied with zero adversarial check — yet hickey and lowy share a model family, a prompt style, and literally cross-reference each other’s catalogs, so their agreement is the correlated, low-signal kind. Route agreed fix findings through the same single cold codex claims-only check before apply. Agreement should gate debate (skip it — the lenses have nothing to argue), never verification.
  • Debate only on disagreement is already the implicit structure (the settled-findings lock); make it explicit and pair it with a tighter backstop — --max-rounds default 3–4 rather than 12. The contested set is monotonically non-increasing, so almost all convergence happens in the first rounds; what’s left at round 4 is a genuine judgment call that unresolved + human adjudication (which /be-review already implements) handles better than rounds 5–12 of bias amplification.
  • Concession reasons here too — same schema addition as /codex-debate: a lens that flips a disposition across rounds must cite the code that convinced it.
  • Keep independent parallel generation, the settled-findings lock, sequential reveal, and the electricity probe — all four survive contact with the literature, and the first is independently validated by it.
  • The cross-lens question itself — “does applying this fix create a problem under the other lens?” — has the only empirically validated catch in the whole adversarial stack ( #198 upstream). The redesign keeps it: in the full tier it lives in the cross-exam rounds as today; in the quick tier it lives in the kill-round’s amend verdict. It must always run with the lens methodology loaded — a generic verifier holding neither SKILL.md cannot run the complecting catalog or the documented hickey/lowy conflict-resolution pattern, which is the part that did the work.

The one-sentence version

Spend the debate budget where disagreement is (contested findings, capped rounds, cited concessions), spend the verification budget where agreement is (cold cross-family kill-rounds on claims-only packets), and never let either budget be spent by a loop whose exit condition is two models getting tired of arguing.

← the Atlas github ↗
